sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 118, level 2.
Valid actions right now: SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'SPACE': 'Changes board state (timer/HUD) but does NOT complete level (VERIFIED steps 32-113)', 'MOUSE': 'Click dot to select (white border), click another dot to swap colors (VERIFIED step 40); cross-rect dot clicks swap colors between red and green rects (VERIFIED step 75)', 'ACTION7': 'Unknown action - gives error when called (VERIFIED step 104)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'top_panel': '7 colored squares at row 1: [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M] - likely target pattern or palette', 'red_rect': 'Red-bordered container at rows 18-25 with 3 paintable dots at cols 21,27,39', 'green_rect': 'Green-bordered container at rows 30-41 with 4 paintable dots at cols 21,27,33,39', 'T_shape': 'Light green (N) connector between red and green rects', 'bottom_squares': '7 gray squares at row 58 - NOT interactive (VERIFIED step 103)'}
- HUD map: {'timer_bar': 'row 53, charcoal bar that changes with SPACE', 'bottom_squares': 'rows 58, gray squares, not interactive'}
- Win pattern: {'unknown': 'SPACE does not complete level even with correct-looking arrangements. All traversal orders tried and failed.'}
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Paint 4 dots with bottom colors, SPACE to submit', 'level_2': 'In progress - 113 actions, 6+ arrangements tried, SPACE never completes'}
- Cross-level notes: {'level_2_mechanic': 'Swapping colors between dots via click-select then click-swap. Cross-rect swapping works.'}
Current level:
- World model: Current: Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]. Tried Green L→R + Red R→L traversal matching top row [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M]. SPACE still fails.
- Goal model: Find correct arrangement or discover mechanic beyond arranging dots. All simple traversal orders exhausted.
- Recent findings: ['ACTION7 gives error', 'Bottom gray squares not interactive', '6 different dot arrangements + SPACE all failed', 'Only SPACE and MOUSE are functional actions']
- Open questions: ["Is there a correct arrangement I haven't tried?", 'Does the goal involve something other than matching the top row?', "Could the T-shape or borders be interactive in a way I haven't tried?", 'Could the white corner pieces be clickable?']
- Plan: Try clicking on white corner pieces of red rect, or try completely different non-arrangement approach
- Strategy log: ['Tried Red L→R + Green L→R: failed', 'Tried Green L→R + Red L→R: failed', 'Tried column-major reading: failed', 'Tried reversed orders: failed', 'Tried T-shape path traversal (Green L→R, Red R→L): failed', 'All 6 arrangement hypotheses exhausted - need fundamentally different approach']
- Failed probes: ['SPACE at step 32 (before all dots painted)', 'ACTION7 (error: unknown action)', 'MOUSE(22,34) click c dot (no effect)', 'MOUSE(8,40) click top N (no effect)', 'SPACE x3 at steps 35-37 (no color shift, no submit)', 'MOUSE(8,10) click top O while dot selected (no effect)', 'SPACE after Red=[O,p,R], Green=[b,N,Y,M] (step 53)', 'SPACE after Green=[O,p,R,b], Red=[N,Y,M]', 'SPACE after Green=[p,O,R,b], Red=[N,Y,M]', 'MOUSE(38,21) then MOUSE(38,27) palette clicks + SPACE', 'MOUSE(58,10) bottom square click (no effect)', 'SPACE after Red=[O,R,Y], Green=[p,b,N,M] (column-major)', 'SPACE after Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b] (T-shape path)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 102 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 3.6x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE while dot selected also didn't change the board (just the timer bar moved). So that's not the mechanic either.
Let me think about this completely differently. I've been assuming the goal is to arrange the 7 colored dots to match the top row. But what if:
1. The goal is NOT about the top row at all
2. The goal is about something else entirely
Let me look at the visual structure again:
- Top: 7 colored squares in a row
- Middle: Red rect (3 dots) connected via T-shape to Green rect (4 dots)
- Bottom: Timer bar and gray squares
What if the top row is NOT a target pattern but rather a set of AVAILABLE COLORS, and the goal is to arrange the dots in some OTHER pattern?
Or what if the goal is to make the dots in each rect match the BORDER color of that rect?
- Red rect border is... actually looking at the image, the border appears to be a dark color (maybe charcoal or dark gray)
- Green rect border is light green (N)
Wait, let me look more carefully at the borders. The red rect has a border that looks like it could be charcoal/dark gray. The green rect has a light green border.
Actually, I notice something: the T-shape connecting the rects is light green (N), and the green rect's border is also light green. The red rect's border appears to be a different color.
Let me reconsider. What if the goal involves:
1. Making all dots in the red rect the SAME color (the border color)
2. Making all dots in the green rect the SAME color (the border color)
But we only have one of each color, so we can't make all dots the same color.
Hmm, what if the goal is to match the dots to the COLORS OF THE RECT BORDERS?
- Red rect border color: need to check
- Green rect border color: need to check
Let me look at the segmentation to find the border colors.
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. What if I need to click on the RECT BORDERS themselves (not the dots, not the corners, but the border lines)?
Or what if the goal is to click on each colored dot in a specific ORDER (like a sequence puzzle)?
Let me try clicking on all 7 dots in the order of the top row: O, p, R, b, N, Y, M
- O is at (36,22) in green rect
- p is at (36,28) in green rect
- R is at (36,34) in green rect
- b is at (36,40) in green rect
- N is at (22,40) in red rect
- Y is at (22,28) in red rect
- M is at (22,22) in red rect
Let me try clicking on them in this sequence.
Actually wait, let me reconsider something. What if the "correct" arrangement is the one where the dots are arranged to match the top row, but read in a DIFFERENT order than I've been trying?
The top row is: O, p, R, b, N, Y, M (left to right)
I've tried:
1. Red L→R + Green L→R: [M,Y,N] + [O,p,R,b] = M,Y,N,O,p,R,b (no)
2. Green L→R + Red L→R: [O,p,R,b] + [M,Y,N] = O,p,R,b,M,Y,N (no, M,Y,N should be N,Y,M)
3. Green L→R + Red R→L: [O,p,R,b] + [N,Y,M] = O,p,R,b,N,Y,M (YES! This matches!)
Wait, I think I DID try this arrangement. Let me check... "Tried T-shape path traversal (Green L→R, Red R→L): failed"
So Green L→R = [O,p,R,b] and Red R→L = [N,Y,M] gives O,p,R,b,N,Y,M which matches the top row. But SPACE didn't complete the level.
Hmm, but what if I had the dots in the wrong positions? Let me check:
- Green L→R: O at (36,22), p at (36,28), R at (36,34), b at (36,40) ✓
- Red R→L: N at (22,40), Y at (22,28), M at (22,22) ✓
So the arrangement IS correct for matching the top row. But SPACE doesn't work. This means either:
1. The goal is NOT to match the top row
2. There's an additional step I'm missing
3. The submission mechanism is different
Let me think about what else could be the submission mechanism...
What if I need to click on the T-shape dot (the charcoal one) AFTER arranging the dots correctly? Or what if I need to click on something else?
Or what if the goal is to arrange the dots in a DIFFERENT order, and the top row is just a palette/reference, not the target order?
Let me try clicking on the charcoal T-shape dot now that the dots are in the "correct" arrangement.
Actually wait, looking at the current state: Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]
- Green L→R: O,p,R,b ✓
- Red R→L: N,Y,M ✓
This matches the top row! But SPACE didn't work.
Let me try clicking on the charcoal T-dot at (22,34) to see if it does something now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# SPACE with dot selected also failed (just timer changed)
# Current arrangement: Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b]
# Green L→R + Red R→L = O,p,R,b,N,Y,M which matches top row
# But SPACE doesn't complete the level

# Let me check the border colors of the rects
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("Border and larger objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] > 20:
        b = n['boundary']
        center_row = sum(r for r,c in b) // len(b)
        center_col = sum(c for r,c in b) // len(b)
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} center=({center_row},{center_col}) children={n.get('children',[])}")

# The dots ARE in the correct arrangement for top row match
# Try clicking the charcoal T-shape dot at (22,34) as submission
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 34}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Only changes timer, never completes level", "MOUSE": "Click dot to select, click another to swap; clicking selected dot deselects", "ACTION7": "Error"}, "object_taxonomy": {"top_panel": "7 colored squares at row 3: [O,p,R,b,N,Y,M]", "red_rect": "3 dots at (22,22)=M,(22,28)=Y,(22,40)=N", "green_rect": "4 dots at (36,22)=O,(36,28)=p,(36,34)=R,(36,40)=b", "T_dot": "charcoal dot at junction between rects"}, "hud_map": {"timer_bar": "row 53"}, "win_pattern": {"unknown": "SPACE never works, need different submission"}, "level_log": {"level_2": "118 actions, all approaches failed"}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Red=[M,Y,N], Green=[O,p,R,b] - matches top row when read Green L→R + Red R→L", "goal_model": "Dots arranged correctly but need different submission method", "recent_findings": ["SPACE with dot selected also just changes timer", "Top row palette doesn't paint dots"], "open_questions": ["What is the submission mechanism?"], "current_plan": "Try clicking T-shape charcoal dot as submission", "strategy_log": ["All arrangements + SPACE failed", "Palette mechanic failed", "White corners failed", "SPACE with selection failed"], "failed_probes": ["All arrangements + SPACE", "ACTION7", "bottom squares", "white corners", "top row palette", "SPACE with dot selected"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

# Let me check the border colors of the rects
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
print("Border and larger objects:")
for n in nodes:
    if n['pixels'] > 20:
        b = n['boundary']
        center_row = sum(r for r,c in b) // len(b)
        center_col = sum(c for r,c in b) // len(b)
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} center=({center_row},{center_col}) children={n.get('children',[])}")

# The dots ARE in the correct arrangement for top row match
# Try clicking the charcoal T-shape dot at (22,34) as submission
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 34}])
